actions.thought: K2 offsuit is a weak starting hand with poor playability, particularly in a 3-handed game where position is valuable but the hand equity is low. Investing 100 chips to call the big blind or raising as a bluff with this specific hand in the first hand of the tournament is negative expected value. A disciplined fold is the most strategic choice to preserve stack depth.

In a blind versus blind situation after the button folds, K9o is a strong enough hand to raise for value and to attempt to take down the blinds. A standard raise size from the Small Blind is typically 3x the Big Blind to put pressure on the opponent and compensate for being out of position post-flop.

76 offsuit is too weak to defend against a 3x raise from the small blind, despite having positional advantage post-flop. Folding is the standard and most mathematically sound decision here to preserve my stack for better spots.

I am on the button with 8h 7d. In 3-handed play, the button should open a wide range of hands to apply pressure on the blinds. While 87o is near the bottom of a standard opening range, it is still a viable candidate for a steal attempt. I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to maintain consistency with my previous hand's sizing and attempt to take down the blinds or play a pot in position.

I have 73 offsuit in the small blind facing a 3x raise from the button. This is a very weak hand with extremely poor playability and equity. Folding is the only reasonable option to preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with 5-3 offsuit, which is a very weak holding. The button has opened, and although the pot odds are somewhat attractive, playing out of position against a range that is likely to be wide but stronger than my own is not a profitable long-term strategy. I prefer to fold and conserve my stack for a better spot.

I am on the Button with pocket sevens (7c, 7d), which is a strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. I want to open with a standard raise to build the pot, take control of the hand, and potentially win the blinds right now. Raising to 2.5x the big blind (250) is a solid sizing.

I hold 8h 4s, which is a very weak starting hand. Facing a raise from the button, playing out of position with such a poor hand is generally a negative expected value (-EV) decision. Folding allows me to preserve my stack and wait for a more advantageous situation.

I am in the Big Blind holding 87o. The Button (Pro) has raised to 250, and the Small Blind has folded. I have already posted 100, so it costs me 150 more to call into a pot of 400, giving me pot odds of 2.67:1. In a 3-handed game, 87o is a standard defending hand from the Big Blind against a button open. While I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, the price is too good to fold, and 3-betting here is unnecessarily risky with a marginal hand this early in the tournament.

On this 6-6-5 flop, I have an open-ended straight draw (7-8). This board structure often favors the Big Blind's range more than the Button's, as I can have many more combinations of 6x and 5x. However, standard play out of position against the pre-flop aggressor is to check to the raiser to see how they react. If Pro bets, I have a very strong draw to continue with.

With pocket sevens on a 6-5-6 board, I have a vulnerable overpair. Flash checked to me. I want to continuation bet for value and protection, as there are many overcards that could hit on the turn and diminish the value of my hand. A smaller sizing of roughly 35-40% pot is standard on paired boards to fold out random overcards while still getting called by worse hands like smaller pocket pairs, 5x, or stubborn Ace-highs.

I have an open-ended straight draw (4s and 9s) on a paired board of 6h-5s-6d. Pro has made a continuation bet of 200 into a pot of 550. I need to call 200 to win a total pot of 750, giving me pot odds of 3.75:1. With 8 clean outs for a straight, I have approximately a 17% chance to hit on the turn. While the pot odds are slightly higher than my direct equity to hit on the next card, the implied odds of winning a larger pot if I hit my straight, combined with the fact that my 8 and 7 might occasionally be the best hand if the board runs out favorably, make calling the most profitable play. I will check-call here to realize my equity without bloating the pot out of position.

On this turn card (4h), I have successfully completed an 8-high straight (4-5-6-7-8). The board is paired with sixes, which introduces some risk of a full house, but a straight is still an extremely strong hand in this three-handed dynamic. Since I am out of position and have been check-calling so far, I will check again to the pre-flop aggressor. This allows him to continue his semi-bluffs or bet with overpairs, providing me an opportunity to check-raise for value or keep the pot manageable if he checks back. Leading out might force him to fold hands that he would otherwise bet.

The turn card is the 4h, putting a potential straight and a flush draw on the board. My pocket sevens are now a mediocre two pair (7s and 6s). Flash check-called the flop, so his range includes a 6, pocket pairs like 88-TT, 5x, and draws like suited hearts or straight draws. Betting here will likely only get called by better hands or strong draws, folding out hands I currently beat. Checking behind controls the pot size, allows me to realize my equity, and gives me a chance to evaluate the river.

On the river King of Clubs, I have an 8-high straight. The board is paired (6-6), but given the action, it is unlikely the opponent has a full house as they checked back the turn. The King is a good card for me to bet because it likely hits the opponent's range (hands like AK, KQ, or KJ) which might have checked the turn for pot control and will now call a value bet. I will bet 600 into the 950 pot to extract value from a King or a mid-pair, as checking again risks the opponent checking back and me missing out on value.

On the river, the board is 6-5-6-4-K. My pocket sevens are now merely a bluff catcher. Flash's flop call and river bet represent a strong range of value hands (any 6, Kx, or higher pocket pairs like 88-JJ) and some bluffs (mostly missed flush draws or straight draws). Because I hold two sevens, I heavily block the 87 straight draw, which is one of the main bluffs he could have. I don't block any missed heart flush draws, but there are plenty of better bluff catchers in my range (like Kx, 88-TT, or a 6). Folding is the disciplined play here.

I am on the button with a pocket pair of threes. This is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. By raising to 300, I take the initiative and have position for the rest of the hand, which allows me to control the pot size and potentially take down the blinds immediately or play the flop with the betting lead.

I am in the Small Blind with 7c 5c. This is a speculative suited gapper. In a 3-handed game, this hand has some value, but playing it out of position against a Button open (Flashlight) is generally disadvantageous. Since Pro (Big Blind) is still to act and could squeeze, calling is risky. While 3-betting as a bluff is an option, I have been very active in the first few hands and don't want to build a large pot out of position with a marginal hand this early in the tournament. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Facing a 3x open from the button, Q7s is a marginal suited hand. While it has some playability and flush potential, the 7 kicker is weak, leading to dominated pair situations out of position against hands like QT, QJ, or KQ. The 3x sizing reduces our pot odds, making a defend less appealing. Folding is the standard, disciplined play to preserve the stack for better spots.

I am on the button with Ace-Nine of spades, which is a premium hand in a three-handed game. Standard strategy dictates a raise to take control of the pot and leverage position. A 3x big blind raise is appropriate here to build the pot while still being able to fold to a significant 3-bet if necessary, though this hand often plays well post-flop.

Facing a 3x open from the Button while in the Small Blind, A6 offsuit is generally too weak to call or 3-bet. Playing out of position with a marginal, easily dominated hand is a long-term losing play. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Three suited. While suited connectors have some playability, Jack-Three is weak and often dominated by the button's opening range. With plenty of tournament life left, folding is the disciplined choice to avoid playing a weak hand out of position in a multi-street pot.
I am on the button with 4d 5h. Offsuit low connectors are very weak, even for a button steal. Folding is the standard and most profitable long-term play here.

6d9s is a weak, uncoordinated offsuit hand. Even though I am in the small blind and the price to complete is relatively cheap (50 to win a pot of 150), playing this hand out of position against the big blind is strategically suboptimal. I prefer to fold and preserve my stack for better opportunities.

T-6 offsuit is a very weak starting hand with poor equity and low playability. It is not profitable to play this hand from the button in an unraised pot, so folding is the standard and most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

With Flashlight folding on the button, I am in the Small Blind with J8 suited, a hand well within the range for a standard opening raise in a blind-vs-blind scenario. I will continue my established sizing of 3x the big blind (300 total) to take the initiative and exert pressure on the Big Blind.

Q7 suited is a marginal hand facing a 3x open from the Small Blind. Even though Flash has been aggressive and opening frequently, calling here with a weak Queen kicker can easily lead to domination post-flop. Folding preserves our stack for better spots.

I am on the button with Ace-Ten offsuit, which is a very strong hand in a 3-handed game. I have a healthy stack and have been successful with standard pre-flop raises in previous hands. Raising to 300 is a standard play here to put pressure on the blinds and potentially take down the pot immediately or play a strong hand with position.

Flash is opening frequently from the Button to 300, but Ten-Nine offsuit is too weak to defend from the Small Blind out of position. Even against a wide range, folding is the standard and safest play here to preserve chips.

I hold 9-3 offsuit, which is a very weak hand with poor playability. Facing a standard button open from Flash, there is no strategic justification for defending my big blind with such a marginal holding. Folding is the most disciplined and optimal play here.

With Q3s on the Button in a 3-handed game, the hand is too weak to justify a standard open raise, especially against an aggressive Big Blind (Flash) who has been defending and 3-betting frequently. A fold is the safest and most optimal play here.

My hand (7-2 offsuit) is the statistically weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Despite the button folding, I am out of position in the small blind against the big blind. There is no strategic value in playing this hand, so folding is the optimal decision to preserve my stack.

My hand (9-3 offsuit) is very weak and has poor equity, even on the button. Investing chips with such a low-value holding is negative EV. Folding is the disciplined action to conserve my stack and wait for a more playable hand.

I have been maintaining an aggressive table image by raising frequently from late positions and the blinds. Pro, the Big Blind, has shown a high frequency of folding to these 3x opens. Although Queen-Trey offsuit is a weak hand, the historical data suggests that a steal attempt here has a high probability of success against this specific opponent. If I face resistance, I can easily fold, but I will continue to apply pressure until the Big Blind adjusts.

Flash is opening to 3bb consistently from the Small Blind when folded to. A9o is a strong hand against a wide steal range and dominates many weaker aces and broadway combos. Given I have positional advantage post-flop, calling is a solid, standard way to defend the Big Blind without bloating the pot unnecessarily against a potentially wide range.

I missed this flop completely with Queen-Trey, but as the pre-flop aggressor, I need to maintain my story of having a strong starting hand. The board of Jack-Nine-Six is coordinated, which hits a Big Blind defending range, but a continuation bet of 300 (half pot) puts the pressure back on Pro to have a piece of it. If Pro calls, I will likely give up on the turn unless the board develops in my favor, but for now, a standard c-bet is the most consistent play to try and win the pot immediately.

On a J-9-6 flop, I have middle pair with top kicker and the Ace of diamonds, which blocks some strong flush draws. Facing a half-pot continuation bet from a wide SB opening range, calling is standard. Raising would isolate us against stronger hands like Jx or two pair, while folding is too nitty given our hand strength and positional advantage.

The turn card is a Jack of Clubs, which pairs the board. While I have been representing a strong hand, this card is actually quite good for my perceived range if I had a Jack or a big overpair. However, since Pro called my flop continuation bet on a wet board (9d-6s-Jd), they likely have a piece of it (a 9, a 6, a flush draw, or a straight draw). The second Jack makes it less likely they have a Jack, but it doesn't necessarily scare them off a 9 or a draw. I have no equity with Queen-high and have already committed 600 chips. Checking here allows me to see what Pro does. If they check back, I might be able to bluff a brick river, but for now, I'm slowing down as my bluff didn't work on the flop.

With two pair (Jacks and Nines) and top kicker, I have a decent made hand, but the paired Jack on the turn reduces my relative hand strength since any J now gives my opponent trips, and overpairs still beat me. Flash checking could be a trap or giving up with draws or weaker pairs. Checking behind allows me to control the pot size with a medium-strength hand, keep his bluffing range wide for the river, and realize my equity safely. Betting would likely fold out worse hands and isolate me against hands that beat me.

The Ace on the river is a significant scare card. However, my decision to check the turn likely capped my perceived range in Pro's eyes, making a bluff on the river less credible. While the Ace is better for my range as the pre-flop aggressor, Pro's call on the flop suggests they have at least a pair or a draw. Given that I have no showdown value with Queen-high, but have already been quite aggressive in previous hands, I will check here. If Pro bets, I will fold, preserving my chip lead rather than risking more on a low-equity bluff.

On the river, the Ace of Spades improves my hand to two pair, Aces and Jacks, with a Nine kicker. However, analyzing the board, if my opponent has any Ace with a Ten or higher kicker (AK, AQ, AT), their kicker will play and beat me. If they have an Ace with a lower kicker (A2-A8), the Nine on the board plays, resulting in a chopped pot. Worse hands like KK, QQ, or a single 9 will likely fold to a bet anyway given the Ace on the river, and stronger hands (like a Jack or a better Ace) will call or raise. Since there are virtually no worse hands that will call and I cannot get value from inferior holdings, checking behind is the safest and most mathematically sound decision.

I am on the button with 9-7 offsuit. In a three-handed game, this hand is strong enough to open for a raise, especially since both opponents in the blinds (Pro and Flashlight) have shown a high frequency of folding to my pre-flop aggression in previous hands. Raising to 300 continues my strategy of applying pressure and taking down the blinds.

Flash is opening to 3bb very frequently from the button and seems to be playing an aggressive, wide range. Holding pocket fives in the Small Blind with nearly 100 BBs deep, we have excellent implied odds to set-mine. Flatting is the standard play here to try and hit a set on the flop, where we can extract maximum value against a loose opponent.

I hold a pocket pair of fours, which plays well in multi-way pots. With a raise to 300 and a call from the small blind, I am getting roughly 3.5:1 pot odds to call. Since I am closing the action in the big blind and have deep stacks, set-mining is the optimal strategy here.

I called pre-flop to set-mine but completely missed the flop. The board of Ace-King-Seven hits the pre-flop raiser's range very hard, and I'm out of position against two opponents. My pair of fives has no value here, so checking with the intention of folding to any bet is the only correct play.

The flop of Ace-King-Seven is terrible for my pocket pair of fours. Both the Ace and the King are high cards that strongly favor the ranges of both the button and the small blind. I have no draws, no overcards, and effectively zero equity if I continue against any aggression. Checking is the only sensible move to minimize losses and re-evaluate if the turn provides any help or if the pot remains checked down.

I have bottom pair on a board with two overcards (Ace and King). As the pre-flop raiser, this board hits my perceived range, but betting into two opponents with only a pair of sevens is high risk for low reward. Checking allows me to control the pot size and utilize my showdown value without being forced to fold to a check-raise. I will see how the turn card changes the board texture before deciding my next move.

With pocket fives on a board of A-K-7-A, my hand is extremely weak and easily beaten by any Ace, King, Seven, or higher pocket pair. There's no value in betting here, and bluffing into two opponents on this texture is risky. I will check and plan to fold to any bet.

I hold a pocket pair of fours on an Ace-high board with two Aces present. With my opponents checking the flop and the small blind checking the turn, there is no betting interest, and my hand has very low showdown value. Betting would only isolate me against stronger hands that would call. Checking is the optimal play to maintain pot control and potentially reach a cheap showdown.

I have a pair of sevens on a board of Ad-Ks-7c-Ac. The turn card is a second Ace, which reduces the likelihood of my opponents holding one, given they have both checked twice. My pair of sevens has some showdown value, but it is vulnerable. By checking behind on the turn, I control the size of the pot and get closer to a cheap showdown. If I bet, I'm unlikely to be called by worse hands, and I might get check-raised by a slow-played monster. Checking allows me to see the river for free and evaluate the final action based on my opponents' behavior.

We've reached the river and the board has completely checked down. With pocket fives, our two pair (Aces and fives) has some showdown value against missed broadway draws or pure air, but there is no value in betting because a worse hand is almost never calling. On the other hand, bluffing is unnecessary and unlikely to succeed against any hand that has us beat (like a 7, a King, or a higher pocket pair) since they would likely call a small to medium bet. Checking to try and reach a cheap showdown is the most prudent move.

My pocket fours have no showdown value on this board of Ace-Ace-King-Seven-Six. The board is too coordinated with high cards for my pair to have any strength, and I cannot realistically represent a hand strong enough to fold out my opponents. Checking is the only logical play here, as it allows me to see the showdown cheaply if everyone else checks, or to easily fold if there is any betting action, thereby preserving my stack.

On this board of Ad-Ks-7c-Ac-6s, I have a pair of sevens which, combined with the pair of Aces on the board and the King, gives me a final hand of A-A-K-9-7. Both opponents have checked on every single street, including the river. This passivity suggests they likely do not have an Ace or a King. While I might have the best hand against air (which plays A-A-K-7-6), there is very little value in betting here. A better hand like a King or a pocket pair is unlikely to fold, and a worse hand is unlikely to call. Checking allows me to reach showdown and win the pot with my marginal holding without risking a check-raise.

I am on the Button with 6s 3c, which is an extremely weak offsuit hand. Even with the advantage of position post-flop, playing a hand with such low equity and zero connectivity is unprofitable. The standard and optimal play is to fold pre-flop.

I am in the small blind and the button has folded, leaving a heads-up situation against the big blind. While Queen-Seven offsuit is a marginal hand, it is standard to complete the small blind in this position to see a flop with a relatively wide range, provided the cost to call is only half a big blind.

I am in the Big Blind with 2s 7d. Flashlight (SB) has completed the blind. There is no reason to raise with one of the weakest possible starting hands, and since checking is free, I will check and see a flop with position on the small blind.

The board of Ace-King-Jack is extremely coordinated and favors the range of the Big Blind, who likely holds many strong Aces, Kings, and straight draws. My hand, Queen-Seven, has very poor equity here, with only a gutshot straight draw if a Ten appears. Betting into this flop would be ineffective as it would rarely fold out better hands. Checking is the optimal play to maintain pot control and evaluate the turn for free or cheap.

I have 7-high on a very coordinated board (Ah-Kh-Jd) that I completely missed. Flashlight checked to me, and since I have no equity and very little chance of successfully bluffing this texture, the best play is to check back and take a free turn.

The turn card (2h) has brought a third heart to the board, making it highly coordinated. I have Queen-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a Ten). Since the flop was checked around, my opponent likely does not have a monster, but they are also likely to have a piece of this broadway-heavy board. Betting into a check-check flop with no made hand is unlikely to be profitable or induce folds. Checking is the most strategic play to maintain pot control and see the river, potentially taking a free card if the opponent checks again.

I have bottom pair on a very coordinated board with three broadway cards and three hearts. While I improved on the turn, my hand remains extremely weak. Flashlight has checked twice, suggesting they likely don't have a strong piece of this board, but betting would turn my hand into a bluff with almost no equity if called. Checking back allows me to take a free card and potentially realize what little showdown value I have without risking more chips on a board that favors an opponent's limping range.

The board is checked down to the river, and I have Queen-high with no pair. My hand has very little showdown value, but since the betting has been non-existent from both sides, a bluff is unlikely to succeed against a range that includes any pair or high card that decided to check back. Checking is the most sensible play to conclude the hand without committing more chips into a likely lost pot.

I have bottom pair (Deuces) on a board that contains an Ace, King, and Jack. While the turn provided me with a pair, this board is extremely dangerous given the straight, flush, and trip possibilities. Flashlight has checked on every street, suggesting a very weak hand or a trap, but since I have showdown value against air and no worse hand will call a bet, checking back is the most logical and safest play to realize my equity without risking further chips.

With Ace-Eight offsuit on the button in a three-handed game, I have a strong enough hand to open the pot. Raising to 300 allows me to leverage my positional advantage, potentially win the blinds immediately, or take the initiative into the post-flop play. This is a standard and aggressive opening move to build a pot.

I have Ten-Nine suited (Td 9d) in the Small Blind. This is a premium suited connector with excellent post-flop playability. Flashlight has opened for 300 from the Button, which is a standard wide range. Calling allows me to see a flop for a relatively cheap price (250 more) and potentially involve the Big Blind for a larger pot. While 3-betting is an option to take the initiative, calling is a safer way to realize equity with this specific hand out of position, given our deep stack sizes.

We hold AQo in the Big Blind facing a Button open and a Small Blind flat call. This is a prime spot for a squeeze play. 3-betting to a large size puts pressure on both opponents, penalizes speculative hands, and seizes the initiative. It often wins the pot pre-flop, and when called, we usually dominate the callers' ranges with our strong Ace-Queen.

Pro has executed a squeeze play from the big blind after I raised on the button and the small blind called. Ace-Eight offsuit is a marginal hand that is likely dominated by the range of hands Pro would choose to 3-bet with in this scenario. Calling 1000 chips into a bloated pot while being out of position against a strong, polarized 3-betting range is negative expected value. Given the depth of my stack and the aggressive action, folding is the most disciplined and profitable play to preserve my capital for a better spot.

Pro has been playing extremely tight over the first 12 hands, folding most of his range. A large squeeze raise to 1,300 from the Big Blind after a button open and my call indicates a very strong range, likely JJ+, AQs+, or AKo. Although 10-9 suited is a premium connector, calling an additional 1,000 chips while out of position against a tight player's premium range is not profitable. I need roughly 34% equity to call, and against a range of JJ+, AK, I have approximately 32% equity and will struggle to realize it out of position. I will fold and preserve my stack.

I am on the button with Kc 6c, a strong suited hand in a 3-handed game. My standard opening size in this tournament has been 300, and I will maintain that consistency here to apply pressure on the blinds and potentially take the pot down pre-flop or play a strong hand in position.
3d 2h is one of the worst hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a 3x raise from the button, there is no justification to play this hand from the small blind. Folding is the only sensible choice.

78o is a weak hand and is difficult to play out of position against an aggressive button raiser. Despite the pot odds, folding is the most disciplined and long-term profitable decision here to preserve my stack.

I am first to act pre-flop on the button in a 3-handed game. My hand, J5 offsuit, is extremely weak and offers poor playability and equity against any calling range. The standard and optimal play is to fold and wait for a better starting hand.

I am in the small blind position, and the button has folded. This leaves me heads-up against the big blind. With queen-six suited, I have a decent enough holding in this blind-vs-blind scenario to complete the bet and see a flop, rather than folding or potentially over-committing with a raise.

I am in the Big Blind with 6d 7s. The Button folded and the Small Blind (Flashlight) just completed. In this situation, I have the option to check and see a free flop or raise to try and take the pot down immediately. Since 76o is a marginal hand and I will have positional advantage over the Small Blind for the remainder of the hand, checking is the most prudent play. It keeps the pot small and allows me to evaluate the flop without committing more chips with a weak starting hand.

I flopped bottom pair (sixes) with a backdoor flush draw in a heads-up blind-vs-blind pot. The board is somewhat connected with a 9, 10, and 6, so betting into my opponent here is risky as it likely only gets called by better hands. Checking allows me to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the turn.

I have bottom pair (sixes) and a gutshot straight draw on a board of 10-9-6. The Small Blind checked to me. While I have a piece of the board, it's a very coordinated texture that likely connects with a lot of the Small Blind's limping range. Checking behind allows me to see a free turn card to either improve to two pair, trips, or a straight, while also preserving my showdown value without bloating the pot with a marginal hand.

I have improved to two pair, Queens and Sixes, on a board of [9c 6h Ts Qd] by pairing my Queen on the turn. My opponent checked both pre-flop (big blind check-option) and on the flop, suggesting they have a relatively weak hand or a draw. With two pair, I have a very strong holding, and I should look to build the pot to extract value from their potential middle pairs, tens, or nines. A bet of 150 into the 200 pot represents a solid value bet sizing that should elicit calls from weaker holdings.

On a board of 10-9-6-Q, my bottom pair of sixes is extremely weak. Flashlight's lead of 150 into a 200 pot after the Queen hits suggests they either have a Queen, a straight, or a strong draw. With many cards in a typical small blind completion range hitting this board (any J, Q, T, or 9), I am likely way behind. Even if I improve to a straight on the river with an 8, I could still be losing to a Jack-high straight. I will fold and preserve my stack.

6-4 offsuit is a very weak starting hand, even in a three-handed game on the button. There is no strategic value in opening with such a marginal hand given my stack size relative to the blinds. I will fold and wait for a more premium hand to engage in the action.

It's folded to me in the Small Blind. Ten-Four suited is a hand with some playability and is a candidate for a blind-vs-blind steal attempt in a 3-handed game. I have been consistently opening for 300 chips throughout the tournament, and maintaining this aggressive image allows me to pick up the blinds frequently. If the Big Blind calls, the suitedness of the hand provides post-flop potential.

I am in the big blind facing a raise to 300 from the small blind (Flash). While Flash has been opening very aggressively, my hand, 3c 5h (unsuited, weak high card, disconnected), is far too weak to defend with, even with the positional advantage post-flop. Folding is the only sensible play here.

I am on the Button with Q4o. While opening ranges on the Button in a 3-handed game are typically wide (40-50%), Q4o is near the bottom of that range and is generally considered a fold. My previous play has been very aggressive, opening frequently to 300, which has been successful in picking up blinds. However, consistently raising with very weak hands like Q4o risks being exploited by opponents who may start 3-betting or calling wider. To maintain a strong table image and avoid playing a potentially dominated hand out of position post-flop, folding is the most prudent play here.

I am in the Small Blind with 2d 5c, which is mathematically one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em. With the Button folding, I am facing the Big Blind. Even in a blind vs. blind scenario, 2-5 offsuit has virtually no playability post-flop and negative expected value to open or limp. Therefore, the optimal play is to fold.

I am on the Button and first to act pre-flop. My hand, 9c 6h (Nine-Six offsuit), is a very weak, disconnected hand with poor playability. The standard and optimal play is to simply fold and wait for a better starting hand.

My hand is 2c Js, which is a very weak, disconnected holding with no significant potential. Being in the small blind puts me out of position for the remainder of the hand, and continuing with such a poor hand would likely result in unnecessary losses. Given my stack size and the strength of the hand, the most disciplined professional decision is to fold and preserve my chips.

I am holding 8c 2d on the button. This is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. With no prospects for a strong pair or straight, and the hand being unsuited and disconnected, playing it would be a negative expected value move. I will fold to preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind and the Button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind. 6c5c is a strong suited connector that plays well in head-up situations. Historically, the Big Blind ('Pro') has folded to several of my pre-flop raises. A standard 3x raise to 300 puts pressure on the BB and can often take the pot down immediately. If called, I have a hand with decent equity and playability post-flop.

Flash has been very active raising to 3bb from the small blind when folded to. With AQo in the Big Blind, I have a premium hand that dominates a significant portion of their wide opening range. A 3-bet here extracts value from worse hands like weaker aces or broadways, punishes steals, and takes the betting initiative. Raising to 900 is a standard 3x 3-bet size.

I am in the Small Blind with 5c6c and faced with a 3-bet from the Big Blind ('Pro') after my initial open. While 5c6c is a strong speculative hand with good playability, calling here puts me in a difficult situation out of position against a player who has shown a willingness to contest pots. Pro's 3-bet suggests a strong range or a calculated adjustment to my opening frequency. I am in the Big Blind with 6d 3s, which is an extremely weak, disconnected, and offsuit hand. Facing a raise from the Button and a call from the Small Blind, I have no equity to justify calling or making a squeeze play. Folding is the only sensible choice to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with King-Seven offsuit on a flop of Jack-Ten-Deuce. My hand is currently just King-high with no pair and no significant draw. I have an open-ended straight draw (needing a 6 or a Jack) on a King-high board with two clubs. Flashlight has made a continuation bet of 350 into a pot of 700. I am getting 3:1 pot odds (calling 350 to win a total pot of 1400), which is favorable given that I have about 17% equity to hit on the turn and 32% by the river. While I am out of position, the implied odds of hitting a straight against a King-heavy range are high enough to justify a call. I will call the 350 to see the turn.

On the turn, I have improved to a pair of Tens and maintain my open-ended straight draw (needing a 6 or a Jack). However, the Ten of Clubs also puts a third club on the board, making a flush possible for my opponent. Since I was the caller on the flop, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor and see how they react to this board texture. Leading out now would likely only be called by better hands or strong draws, so I will check to realize my equity and evaluate based on their action.

I have hit my flush on the turn with the Jack of clubs and Five of clubs, as the Ten of clubs hit the board. With a made flush in position against a checked opponent, I should extract value. A bet of 600 into a 1300 pot is a solid sizing to get called by weaker pairs or straight draws while keeping the pot growing.

I have improved to middle pair (tens) and still hold an open-ended straight draw (any Jack or Six completes the straight). The turn also brought a third club, but I hold the Nine of clubs, which is a key blocker to some of Flashlight's potential flushes. Flashlight has bet 600 into a pot of 1400, giving me pot odds of approximately 3.3:1. My combined equity from the straight draw (approx. 17%) and the possibility that a pair of tens is currently the best hand or can win at showdown makes calling the most mathematically sound decision. Raising is unnecessary and would likely only be called by better hands.

On the river, the 5 of hearts is a complete blank that did not help my open-ended straight draw. I currently hold middle pair (tens) on a board containing a King and three clubs. This hand has some showdown value against missed draws or lower pairs, but it is unlikely to be called by anything worse if I bet. Therefore, checking is the best course of action to reach showdown as cheaply as possible or to potentially catch a bluff from a missed straight draw.

I have successfully made a Jack-high flush on the river, as the board contains three clubs (Kc, 7c, Tc) and I hold the Jc. The opponent checked, signaling weakness or a missed draw. Given the pot size is 2500, a value bet is the optimal strategy to extract additional chips from a pair or a weaker hand that might feel compelled to call. I will bet 1200, which is approximately half the pot, a sizing that balances value extraction with the risk of the opponent folding.

On the river, the 5 of hearts did not change the board texture significantly. I am holding middle pair (tens) with a 9 of clubs blocker. Flashlight has triple-barreled, betting 300 pre-flop, 350 on the flop, 600 on the turn, and now 1200 on the river. This line is very strong and typically represents a King, a flush, or a straight. While my Ten of Diamonds provides some showdown value against a pure bluff, calling a large river bet against a consistent aggressor with only middle pair is risky. The pot odds are roughly 3.1:1, meaning I need to be right more than 24% of the time. Given Flashlight's persistent aggression across all streets, it is more likely they are betting for value rather than bluffing with a hand I can beat. I will fold to preserve my stack for a more favorable opportunity.

I am on the button with 8s 3s. While being suited provides some playability, 8-3 is a very weak starting hand even for a steal attempt from the button, especially against active opponents who have shown a willingness to defend and 3-bet. Given the recent loss in Hand #85, I will play disciplined and fold this marginal hand to wait for a better spot.

Ace-Ten suited is a premium hand in a Blind vs Blind situation. With the Button folding, I will raise to 3x the big blind (300) to build the pot, take the initiative, and deny free equity to the Big Blind.

I am in the big blind with a weak hand, 5h 3h. A call of 200 to win a total pot of 400 is not mathematically favorable given the poor board coverage and strength of this hand. It is safer to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with a very weak suited connector, 3d4d. The most profitable and lowest-variance play here is to simply fold and wait for a better spot to attack.

I am in the small blind with 5d 6c. While this is a weak hand, the pot odds in a heads-up small blind vs. big blind scenario make completing (calling the additional 50 chips) a standard, low-variance play to see a flop and potentially realize some equity, rather than folding and surrendering the small blind immediately.

I am in the Big Blind with Qd 9c. The Button (Pro) has folded, and the Small Blind (Flashlight) has just completed the blind. While I could consider a raise to take down the pot immediately or isolate, checking allows me to see a flop for free with a hand that has decent high-card value and potential to connect. Given the stack sizes and the tournament stage, I'll take the free flop.

I have bottom pair on a fairly coordinated board (Jack high, two diamonds). With a weak kicker, I want to keep the pot small and avoid inflating it. Checking allows me to evaluate Flash's response. If Flash bets small, I can reconsider my options, but betting into them here doesn't serve much purpose as it folds out hands worse than mine and gets called by better ones.

On this flop of Jh 6d 8d, I have Queen-high with one overcard to the board and a backdoor straight draw. The Small Blind (Flashlight) checked after completing pre-flop. In a limped pot, my Queen-high still has some showdown value against a wide small blind range. Checking back allows me to see the turn for free, keeping the pot small while retaining the possibility of improved equity or a bluff on later streets if the board develops favorably.

The board (Jh 6d 8d 9d) is highly coordinated, creating numerous straight and flush possibilities. Holding a pair of sixes (5d 6c), my hand is quite vulnerable. Since I am first to act on the river, checking is the most prudent strategy to maintain pot control and realize any potential showdown value without risking further chips on a weak hand.

I have improved to a pair of nines and also hold the Queen of Diamonds, giving me a flush draw on this three-diamond board. The Small Blind has checked for the third time (pre-flop, flop, and turn), suggesting a weak range or a trap. In this small, limped pot, my pair of nines has decent showdown value, and my flush draw provides significant equity if the river is a diamond. Checking back allows me to see the river for free, realize my equity, and avoid a potential check-raise on a highly coordinated board.

The board texture is [Jh 6d 8d 9d 7h], creating a four-card straight on the board (6-7-8-9-J). My hand [5d 6c] provides the 5, completing a low straight (5-6-7-8-9). Betting into a board this coordinated, where I hold only the bottom end of a straight, offers little value as I will only be called by better hands (tens) or split pots (other fives), while betting may fold out hands that might check behind. Checking is the most prudent action to reach showdown safely.

On this highly coordinated board (Jh 6d 8d 9d 7h), my pair of nines has some showdown value in a limped pot, but it is vulnerable to many hands. A Ten or a Five would have made a straight, and any two diamonds would have made a flush. Since Flashlight has checked for the third time, he is likely weak or potentially trapping with a marginal hand. Given the small pot size (200) and the high probability that a bet only gets called by better hands or folds out hands I already beat, checking back to realize my showdown value is the most prudent play.

My hand is 5-3 offsuit, which is extremely weak even in position on the button. There is no incentive to invest chips into this pot with such a poor holding. Folding is the standard and correct play to preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with A9o. Since the Button has folded, it is down to me and the Big Blind. A9o is a strong hand in a head-to-head battle between blinds. Facing a 3x open from the Small Blind, 98o is too weak of a holding to defend, even with position post-flop. I'll fold and wait for better spots to commit chips.

96o is a weak offsuit hand, definitely not worth playing from the button or anywhere else in a 3-handed game. I will fold to conserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind holding ATo, which is a very strong hand in a blind-vs-blind scenario. Flashlight, in the Small Blind, has merely completed the bet. I am in the small blind holding Ten-Seven offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Flash has raised the big blind to 400, which is a significant raise (4x the big blind). I am on the button with a suited connector-like hand, which has decent potential for a steal or post-flop play. With the blinds at 50/100 and no prior action, raising to 300 is a standard button open to take the initiative and potentially win the blinds immediately.

I am in the Small Blind with 6h 4h, which is a weak speculative hand. Flashlight opened for 3x from the button. AJo is a very strong hand 3-handed, especially against a Button open. 3-betting from the Big Blind applies pressure, charges the opponent to see a flop, and frequently wins the pot immediately. It also denies equity to speculative hands in the Button's wide opening range.

I am on the button with Jack-Ten offsuit, which is a strong starting hand in three-handed play. I am in the Big Blind with a weak hand (10 of Hearts, 2 of Clubs). The Button has folded and the Small Blind has simply called. There is no reason to raise with such a marginal hand; I will check to see a flop for free and preserve my stack.

Defending my Big Blind with suited connectors (6c 4c) against a button raise. The price is correct (200 to call into a pot of 450), and the hand has decent post-flop playability, making a call the most strategically sound decision.

I am on the Button with 8h 2s, which is an extremely weak offsuit hand, one of the worst in Texas Hold'em. There is no strategic justification for entering the pot with this hand, even from late position. Folding is the only optimal play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the button position with 6-3 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Even though the cost to call is only 100, playing this hand OOP or in this position is statistically negative expectation given the lack of playability and connectivity. I will fold.

I have 94 offsuit on the button, which is a very weak hand. The standard play here is to fold and wait for a better opportunity.
